text.title: Post-Webinar Follow-up Sequence
text.summary: 5-email follow-up structure with timing and subject purpose; conversion metrics benchmarks; common delivery and follow-up mistakes.

section.title: 5-Email Sequence Structure

rule.statement: Send 5 emails over 5 days: immediate (replay + offer), Day 1 (key takeaways + offer), Day 2 (case study + testimonial), Day 3 (FAQ + deadline reminder), Day 5 (deadline extension if used). Each email must carry the offer with a deadline.
rule.rationale: Most conversions happen on Day 1–3 but a meaningful portion (15–25%) come from Day 3–5 when urgency of the deadline becomes real. A single "replay available" email leaves this conversion window unused.

section.reference: Immediate email: replay link, slides, offer CTA.
Day 1 email: key takeaways, bonus resources, offer.
Day 2 email: case study, testimonial, deadline.

list.item: Show-up rate: good 40%, great 60%+. Below 30% means registration-to-event delay is too long or reminder sequence is weak.
Watch time: good 60% of duration, great 80%+. Below 50% means delivery is losing audience — audit first 15 minutes.
Conversion (attendees to buyers): good 5%, great 15%+. Below 2% means offer mismatch or weak CTA delivery.
Email open rate (follow-up sequence): good 30%, great 50%+.

antipattern.description: Spending more than 20% of the webinar on the pitch or offer presentation.
antipattern.reason: Audiences register for the educational content. Sensing a bait-and-switch causes early dropoff. The 80/20 rule (80% value, 20% pitch) is the maximum promotional tolerance for a live audience.

antipattern.description: Sending only a "replay available" email and no follow-up sequence.
antipattern.reason: Single-email post-webinar follow-up typically converts 1–2% of attendees. The 5-email sequence converts 5–15% by serving different buyer readiness stages on different days.
Without urgency, "I'll think about it" means "never". A concrete deadline (72 hours, specific date) converts fence-sitters by making inaction costly.
